officials.name: Donald Trump
officials.title: President of the United States

contact.office: The White House, 1600 Pennsylvania Ave NW, Washington, DC 20500

alignment.label: Antagonist
alignment.summary: Significant misalignment on housing regulation, climate, and governance philosophy. Some narrow overlap on deregulation of building permits and infrastructure rhetoric, but overall approach conflicts with evidence-based, equity-conscious policymaking.
key_agreements: Rhetoric around cutting red tape for construction and permitting
General pro-development stance in some contexts
key_disagreements: Gutting federal housing programs and HUD funding
Undermining transit investment and climate-linked infrastructure
Anti-immigration policies that reduce housing demand modeling accuracy
Erosion of government transparency and institutional norms
alignment.engagement_strategy: Minimal direct engagement value. Focus energy on state and local officials where your vote and voice carry more weight. Monitor federal policy changes that impact local housing funding (CDBG, LIHTC) and transit grants (FTA). Advocate through national orgs like California YIMBY or YIMBY Action for federal housing reform.

officials.notes: Federal executive sets broad policy direction but limited direct impact on Austin zoning and land use.

officials.name: JD Vance
officials.title: Vice President of the United States

contact.office: The White House, 1600 Pennsylvania Ave NW, Washington, DC 20500

alignment.label: Antagonist
alignment.summary: Has expressed some pro-housing supply views in the past but overall policy direction conflicts with pragmatic urbanism and evidence-based governance.
key_agreements: Has acknowledged housing supply constraints publicly
key_disagreements: Social conservatism conflicts with inclusive urbanism
Populist economic framing conflicts with market-oriented housing reform
Anti-institutional stance undermines good-government goals
alignment.engagement_strategy: No direct engagement value. Senate tie-breaking role is the only procedural lever — track any housing-related Senate votes that could go 50-50.

contact.office: Office of the Governor, P.O. Box 12428, Austin, TX 78711

alignment.label: Mostly Opposed
alignment.summary: State preemption of Austin's progressive policies is a recurring friction point. However, some deregulatory moves (e.g., limiting local zoning power) accidentally align with YIMBY goals.
key_agreements: Some deregulation of local zoning restrictions could ease housing construction
key_disagreements: State preemption of Austin's local governance (e.g., plastic bag bans, police oversight)
Hostile to transit funding and climate-linked infrastructure
Anti-urban bias in state policy and funding allocation
Undermines local democratic processes Austin relies on
alignment.engagement_strategy: Engage selectively on areas of accidental alignment (housing deregulation). Push back through state-level advocacy orgs on preemption. The governor's office is responsive to business-community framing — lead with economic arguments, not progressive values.

alignment.label: Strong Antagonist
alignment.summary: Controls the Texas Senate agenda and has consistently blocked urban-friendly legislation. Anti-transit, anti-density, and hostile to Austin's political priorities.
key_agreements: Property tax reform rhetoric (though implementation often hurts cities)
key_disagreements: Blocks transit funding legislation
Pushes anti-urban state preemption bills
Social conservatism conflicts with inclusive urbanism
Uses Lt. Gov power to kill housing reform bills in committee
alignment.engagement_strategy: Very limited direct engagement value. The Lt. Governor controls the Senate calendar — the most powerful figure in Texas government. Work through business-friendly Republican senators who might carry housing-adjacent bills. Frame everything in terms of property rights and economic growth, never progressive values.

contact.office: Travis County Administration Building, 700 Lavaca St, Austin, TX 78701

alignment.label: Ally
alignment.summary: Pragmatic Democrat aligned on housing, transit, and fiscal responsibility. Pro-development within a social equity framework. Chairs Commissioners Court.
key_agreements: Fiscal moderate — balances investment with budget discipline
key_disagreements: May prioritize affordability mandates over total housing supply in some cases
County bureaucratic processes can slow development
alignment.engagement_strategy: High-value engagement target. County Judge chairs Commissioners Court and sets the agenda. Attend public comment sessions, especially on housing and transit bond items. Frame housing supply as complementary to affordability — they're not opposed. Email directly with specific policy asks.

contact.office: Austin City Hall, 301 W 2nd St, Austin, TX 78701

alignment.label: Strong Ally
alignment.summary: Watson is the pragmatic anchor of Austin's pro-development coalition. Former state senator with deep institutional knowledge. Pro-housing, pro-transit, fiscally disciplined. The most aligned senior official in your jurisdiction.
key_agreements: Supports streamlined permitting and development review
Fiscal discipline — balances progressive goals with budget reality
key_disagreements: Occasionally cautious on pace of change — pragmatism can mean incrementalism
Convention center decision was controversial
alignment.engagement_strategy: Your most important ally. Support Watson's housing agenda publicly at council meetings. Submit public comments on HOME initiative implementation items. The mayor's office is responsive to constituent emails with specific, data-backed asks. Volunteer for housing-related task forces when openings arise.

alignment.label: Antagonist
alignment.summary: Deeply misaligned on governance philosophy, climate, and social issues. Narrow potential alignment on deregulation and housing supply rhetoric but no legislative track record on housing reform.
key_agreements: Occasional pro-deregulation rhetoric that could benefit housing
key_disagreements: Voted against infrastructure bills that fund transit
Anti-climate policy undermines sustainable development
Culture war focus diverts from pragmatic governance
Opposed federal housing funding increases
alignment.engagement_strategy: Very limited. Your one lever: constituent pressure on specific votes. When infrastructure or housing bills hit the Senate floor, call the Austin district office. Frame in terms of Texas economic competitiveness, not progressive values. Don't waste time on culture-war issues.

alignment.label: Mostly Opposed
alignment.summary: More pragmatic than Cruz but still significantly misaligned. Has occasionally crossed party lines on infrastructure. Worth monitoring for specific votes.
key_agreements: More willing to negotiate than Cruz
key_disagreements: Generally opposes federal housing mandates
Fiscal conservatism extends to opposing transit funding
Doesn't prioritize urban issues
alignment.engagement_strategy: Slightly more reachable than Cruz. When bipartisan infrastructure or housing votes come up, contact the Austin office. Cornyn responds to business-community pressure — coordinate with Austin Chamber and tech sector contacts. His IIJA vote shows he can be pragmatic.

alignment.label: Ally
alignment.summary: Long-serving Democrat generally aligned on progressive housing and transit goals. More populist than Doug's preferred technocratic approach, but substantively aligned on most policy outcomes.
key_agreements: Supports federal housing investment and tenant protections
Champions environmental protection tied to development
key_disagreements: More populist framing than Doug prefers
May prioritize affordability mandates over supply-side solutions
Sometimes prioritizes symbolic votes over pragmatic deal-making
alignment.engagement_strategy: Valuable constituent relationship. Attend town halls — Doggett holds them regularly. Push for pro-supply housing positions at the federal level (YIMBY Act, zoning reform incentives). Frame housing supply as complementary to affordability. His Austin district office is responsive to specific policy asks.

contact.office: Capitol Extension, Room E1.806, Austin, TX 78701

alignment.label: Strong Ally
alignment.summary: Former Travis County Judge, deeply familiar with local governance. Pro-housing, pro-transit, pragmatic approach to state policy. One of the most aligned state legislators for YIMBY priorities.
key_agreements: Supports housing supply reform at state level
Pro-transit investment including Project Connect support
Fights state preemption of Austin's local authority
key_disagreements: In the minority party — limited legislative power
May lean more progressive on some economic regulation than Doug prefers
alignment.engagement_strategy: High-value relationship. As a minority-party senator, she needs constituent support to build coalitions with moderate Republicans on specific bills. Contact her office during session (odd years) on housing and transit bills. Attend interim hearings on housing topics. She's a former county judge — responsive to data-driven, pragmatic framing.

officials.name: TBD
officials.title: State Representative, HD-51

alignment.label: Unknown
alignment.summary: Current representative needs verification. HD-51 covers central/east Austin — typically a progressive Democrat.
alignment.engagement_strategy: Verify current representative and research their positions on housing and transit. State reps are the most accessible state-level officials — attend town halls and build a direct relationship.

officials.notes: Verify current member for HD-51. Texas Legislature meets in odd-year sessions.

officials.name: Margaret Gómez (retiring)
officials.title: Travis County Commissioner, Precinct 4

contact.office: Travis County Administration Building, 700 Lavaca St, Austin, TX 78701

alignment.label: Mixed
alignment.summary: Retiring after holding this seat since 1995. May 26 runoff between Morales and Ledesma-Woody will determine successor. Gómez's long tenure means institutional knowledge leaving — engage with both runoff candidates now.
key_agreements: Generally supportive of social services and affordable housing
key_disagreements: Slow-moving institutional approach may frustrate pro-development goals
Less focused on housing supply than demand-side solutions
alignment.engagement_strategy: Lame duck — engage with runoff candidates instead. Research George Morales III and Susanna Ledesma-Woody on housing, transit, and fiscal positions. The May 26 runoff has ~8-12% expected turnout — your vote and volunteer time have outsized impact.

officials.notes: RETIRING. May 26 runoff: George Morales III (40.17%) vs Susanna Ledesma-Woody (35.18%). No Republican running — winner takes seat.

officials.name: Natasha Harper-Madison
officials.title: Austin City Council Member, District 1

contact.office: Austin City Hall, 301 W 2nd St, Austin, TX 78701

alignment.label: Ally
alignment.summary: Generally supportive of housing development and equity initiatives. Pro-density, pro-transit. Term-limited — open seat in Nov 2026.
key_agreements: Supports housing development and density in District 1
key_disagreements: Sometimes prioritizes equity framing over pure supply arguments
Pace of action can be slower than desired on development approvals
alignment.engagement_strategy: YOUR direct council representative — highest-impact local relationship. Attend District 1 town halls. Submit public comments on housing items. Email the district office with specific asks. She's term-limited (Jan 2027), so focus on getting key votes through before the transition. Start building a relationship with her successor candidates.

officials.notes: Term-limited Jan 2027. Open seat election Nov 3, 2026. Declared candidates: Steven Brown, Alexandria Anderson.

alignment.label: Neutral
alignment.summary: School board has indirect impact on housing through school quality perceptions and facility decisions. AISD faces enrollment decline as families are priced out — aligned on need for more affordable housing.
key_agreements: Shared interest in affordable housing to maintain enrollment
key_disagreements: School districts sometimes oppose density near schools
Bond spending priorities may not align with housing/transit focus
alignment.engagement_strategy: Lower priority but relevant for housing narrative. AISD's enrollment decline strengthens the case for more housing. Cite AISD data when advocating for density — more families need to be able to afford to live in Austin for schools to thrive.

officials.name: Capital Metro Board
officials.title: Transit Authority Board

alignment.label: Strong Ally
alignment.summary: Overseeing Project Connect light rail implementation — the most transformative infrastructure project in Austin. Board is generally pro-transit expansion and aligned with density-supporting development.
key_disagreements: Implementation pace and cost overruns are a concern
TX Supreme Court ruling on Project Connect funding still pending
alignment.engagement_strategy: Attend monthly board meetings and public comment sessions. Project Connect is the single biggest infrastructure question for Austin — public support at board meetings matters. Push for TOD (transit-oriented development) policies that maximize housing near future stations. Monitor TX Supreme Court ruling on Project Connect funding.

officials.notes: TX Supreme Court ruling on Project Connect funding remains pending.
